label: JVC Jazz Festival: For the Love of Louis

quotes.speaker: Randy Sandke
quotes.text: Yeah. I mean I don’t know that that’s so much a generational thing because I think it’s always been like that. There’ve been a few players that can just make it sing and take wings and whatever, and a lot of people for whatever reason, it’s just less than that. And there are some players these days that I think are quite lyrical, and even younger players, and then guys who are not. One thing, going back to another thing about playing, one thing that sort of interests me, I mean I guess is that Louis Armstrong for instance, he always, he could play with the worst band in the world and at times — not the worst band but guys that were just on a totally different — I mean nobody was on his level, but I mean like way under. And he would say that he didn’t even hear the band. Like he would just play, he just had such a strong feeling inside him that he would just charge ahead and it would just fall into place. And he could do that. And of course, that’s him. I cannot do that. I mean I can do that to a certain extent, but I always feel very much like I’m playing WITH the guys and that my playing is affected, for better or worse, by what’s going on around me. And sometimes I feel like it’s very difficult for me to like really get it happening if it doesn’t seem like it is around me. You know I’ll try, but I mean I guess that’s the nature of the thing. Sometimes you feel like it’s work, other times it’s just happening by itself. And it doesn’t necessarily, there are so many variables. It’s not just even the caliber of the musicians. The night after last we were doing this thing at Carnegie Hall and I played a few small group things. The rhythm section was terrific. Some of the tunes felt really good. The last tune we did, it just felt like — I mean I don’t know if the audience perceived this. But it just felt like for whatever reason it just was not coming together, it was an effort all the way through. And it sounded okay, I mean nobody did any obvious goofs. But to me it just didn’t really have the feeling. It wasn’t comfortable. And of course like when you don’t feel comfortable, there’s always a tendency to blame yourself. I think when you’ve got experience you tend to notice a little bit more that it’s other things too. But you just get into so many variables — the weather, how people are feeling, the acoustics.
quotes.caption: Randy Sandke recalls a difficult small group performance at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Randy Sandke interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997
